name: OFDMA Resource Block Scheduling
description: Sub-sector template for OFDMA resource block allocation and scheduling patents. Covers physical resource block assignment, multi-user OFDMA scheduling, CSI-based resource allocation, and frequency-selective scheduling.
contextDescription: OFDMA resource block scheduling is the core physical-layer scheduling mechanism in LTE and 5G NR. Every base station must implement RB allocation. High-value targets include Qualcomm (modem IP), Apple (baseband integration), Samsung (infrastructure + devices), and Intel (former modem division). SEP potential is very high — 3GPP TS 36.213 and 38.214 define RB scheduling procedures. Key differentiators: multi-user pairing efficiency, CSI feedback utilization, sub-band granularity, and carrier aggregation scheduling across component carriers.
scoringGuidance: OFDMA RB scheduling is mandatory in every LTE/5G device — enormous infringement surface.
Multi-user OFDMA (MU-OFDMA in WiFi 6/7, MU-MIMO scheduling in 5G) patents have the broadest impact.
CSI-based scheduling patents that reduce feedback overhead are particularly valuable for massive MIMO.
Carrier aggregation scheduling across multiple CCs is increasingly important for 5G-Advanced.
Patents covering both uplink and downlink scheduling have broader licensing scope.

questions.question: At what granularity does this patent perform resource scheduling? Score 8-10 for sub-PRB or symbol-level granularity (5G NR mini-slots), 5-7 for PRB-level scheduling across subframes, 3-4 for subband-level, 1-2 for wideband-only scheduling.
questions.reasoningPrompt: Identify the scheduling granularity in time and frequency. Finer granularity enables better resource utilization.

questions.question: Does this patent enable simultaneous multi-user scheduling (MU-OFDMA, MU-MIMO pairing)? Score 8-10 for dynamic MU pairing with interference management, 5-7 for basic multi-user scheduling, 3-4 for single-user scheduling with multi-user awareness, 1-2 for single-user only.
questions.reasoningPrompt: Multi-user scheduling multiplies capacity and is central to 5G NR and WiFi 6/7.

questions.question: How does this patent use channel state information (CSI) for scheduling decisions? Score 8-10 for novel CSI compression, prediction, or differential feedback mechanisms, 5-7 for standard CSI-based frequency-selective scheduling, 3-4 for CQI-only decisions, 1-2 for no CSI usage.
questions.reasoningPrompt: CSI feedback is the bottleneck for massive MIMO scheduling. Innovations here have high SEP value.

questions.question: How closely does this patent align with 3GPP standard procedures for resource scheduling (TS 36.213/38.214)? Score 8-10 for direct implementation of standardized scheduling procedures or mandatory UE/gNB behavior, 5-7 for techniques that enable standard compliance, 3-4 for standard-adjacent but implementation-specific, 1-2 for proprietary approaches with no standard relevance.
questions.reasoningPrompt: SEP-aligned patents can be licensed to all implementers of the standard. Identify specific 3GPP procedures if possible.

questions.question: Does this patent address scheduling across multiple component carriers (carrier aggregation)? Score 8-10 for cross-carrier scheduling optimization or dynamic CC activation, 5-7 for per-CC scheduling with CA awareness, 1-3 for single carrier only.
questions.reasoningPrompt: Carrier aggregation is mandatory for LTE-Advanced and 5G NR high-throughput modes.

questions.question: Does this patent cover uplink scheduling, downlink scheduling, or both? Score 8-10 for unified UL/DL scheduling framework or TDD dynamic slot allocation, 5-7 for comprehensive single-direction scheduling, 3-4 for basic single-direction, 1-2 for very narrow scope.
questions.reasoningPrompt: Patents covering both directions have twice the licensing scope.

questions.question: How detectable is this scheduling innovation in a commercial product? Score 8-10 for behavior observable in over-the-air signaling (DCI formats, scheduling grants), 5-7 for behavior inferable from UE throughput patterns or standard conformance testing, 3-4 for internal baseband processing only detectable via chip analysis, 1-2 for algorithm-level with no external observability.
questions.reasoningPrompt: Detectability is critical for enforcement. Over-the-air observable scheduling decisions are easiest to prove.
